title: V: Data processing
description: {"blocks":[{"key":"vshv","text":"This section provides an overview of analytic approaches that are commonly applied to PhIP-Seq data. We also outline data processing workflow for TIU PanSeq libraries.","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}}],"entityMap":{}}
steps: The output of a PhIP-Seq experiment is a set of raw sequencing files derived from individual PhIP-Seq immunoprecipitations. Thus, to convert this output into measures of immunoreactivity, the sequencing files must be processed and evaluated. Here, we outline early data processing steps common to PhIP-Seq workflow ( Figure 1 ). Later steps of data analysis and interpretation are very diverse, application-specific, and their description lies outside of the scope of this document. Figure 1. Overview of the TIU data processing workflow Following sequencing and read demultiplexing, the first step is to align sequences to the proper reference sequence set and to generate a per-tile read count file. We use bwa-mem2 ( https://github.com/bwa-mem2/bwa-mem2 ) to align sequencing data to the appropriate reference oligo library file (the human or the exposure library). Following alignment, we generate a count table using the samtools idxstats command ( https://github.com/samtools ). This results in a set of per-sample files containing the number of sequencing reads aligned to each tile. This initial data processing is accompanied by both raw read data QC (quality control) and alignment-level QC. We use FastQC ( https://github.com/s-andrews/FastQC ) to evaluate raw sequencing data. This basic QC includes evaluation of sequencing yield, ensuring that we obtained sufficient sequencing depth for adequate statistical power of the analysis (usually, > 1x coverage, or ca. ~1M reads for our libraries). Independent of raw yield is the evaluation of compositional diversity ( Figure 2 ). For human libraries, we normally expect high-complexity libraries ( Figure 2 , left). Low-complexity patterns ( Figure 2 , right) may indicate the presence of gross contaminants (e.g., adapters). For exposure libraries, though, we often observe low-complexity patterns as the libraries possess highly biased composition with several strongly overrepresented sequences. Subsequent alignment-level QC includes an estimation of error rates and other alignment statistics. Aberrant statistics may point towards failed library prep or sequencing failure. Figure 2. Examples of high-complexity (left) and low-complexity (right) PhIP-Seq libraries. FastQC analysis of raw reads Following initial processing, there is a diverse set of downstream analytic approaches that can be employed depending on the specific PhIP-Seq libraries being used and the questions being addressed. Some of the commonly used approaches are the identification of enriched peptides in a single sample, cohort-level comparisons, or the identification of enriched pathogen groups in the data (See Sundell and Tao, 2024 for an overview of commonly used statistical approaches). For a single-sample report, the output is often compared to the background distribution pattern in mock IP samples (bead controls) or a healthy control group. We prefer healthy controls as a reference when working with human biofluids because the background distribution of peptide enrichments differs between mock IP samples. We have observed IgG-independent peptide reactivity resulting using IgG-depleted serum to immunoprecipitate PanSeq libraries. Subsequently, the peptide counts are normalized, and enriched peptides are identified using statistical tests such as the negative-binomial test or Bayesian approaches (See Sundell and Tao, 2024 for overview, or for Chen et al., 2022 and Galloway et al., 2023 for specific examples). Peptide-level read counts can be integrated over technical replicates, if available, or integrated at the gene level. Further, peptide sequences can be used to infer enriched binding motifs. A variety of approaches can and are used. The appropriate approach and statistical tests depend on the experimental design and data structure. For cohort-level analysis, a common question is whether there are sets of enriched peptides/genes in one cohort (e.g., disease state) compared to the other (“healthy controls”). This basic structure can be extended to include multiple cohorts (disease states) or be more complicated. A basic analytic approach can start with the appropriate single-sample analysis, followed by output integration and application of cohort-level tests on a per-peptide or per-gene level, or be intrinsically based on the use of multiple samples ( Sundell and Tao, 2024 ). Such approaches could include supervised or non-supervised sample clustering or application of other machine-learning approaches to sample classification (for example, Kung et al., 2025 ) Analysis of pathogen exposure data is largely similar to human data, and a variety of approaches have been used. Unlike human autoreactivity data, though, end goals of the analysis tend to be different, and rather than focusing on the identification of auto-reactive antibodies, cohort-level analysis is more common. For low-frequency, uncommon pathogens, the analysis is conceptually similar to human auto-antibody reactivity analysis, whereby your baseline expectation of observing enriched peptides to a rare pathogen is low. For common pathogens, though, care should be taken to account for the possibility of high-frequency reactivity. Practically, it means that certain peptides would not be enriched compared to a set of healthy controls. The analysis could be further extended to integrate human and exposure PhIP-Seq data or cross-referenced with other independent measures. Subsequently, questions such as molecular mimicry effects in immune response or other more advanced questions can be addressed.
For peer-reviewed manuscripts that reference the PanSeq library, the specific analytic approach will be detailed within the methods section of the manuscript.
creator: Pavel Khil
